title: Best VPN for Russia in 2026: What Still Works After 439 Services Were Blocked
description: Roskomnadzor blocked 439 VPN services. Russia's TSPU DPI boxes run on every ISP. Standard OpenVPN and WireGuard are dead. Here's what protocol actually bypasses Russian filtering in 2026.
content:
## The Scale of Russia's VPN Crackdown

Russia's internet filtering program has been building for years, but 2025 and 2026 mark a significant escalation. Roskomnadzor, the federal telecom regulator, has blocked 439 VPN services as of early 2026, a 70% jump from 12 months earlier. The Russian government has allocated roughly 60 billion rubles (approximately $780 million USD) to its 2025-2027 VPN blocking program, including over 2 billion rubles specifically for AI-powered traffic analysis.

This isn't just a blocklist of IP addresses anymore. Russia has deployed physical hardware at every major ISP.

## How Russia Actually Blocks VPNs: The TSPU System

The core of Russia's filtering infrastructure is called TSPU (Технические средства противодействия угрозам, or "Technical Means of Countering Threats"). These are DPI appliances that Russian law requires every ISP to install and connect to Roskomnadzor's centralized control system.

TSPU boxes sit inline on ISP traffic and can:
- Analyze packet payloads in real-time
- Identify protocol signatures regardless of port (OpenVPN looks like OpenVPN whether it's on 1194 or 443)
- Execute throttling or blocking commands pushed from Roskomnadzor's central system within minutes
- Apply machine learning-based traffic classification to catch obfuscated protocols

Since late 2025, standard OpenVPN, WireGuard, and most TLS-based proxy protocols are blocked at the TSPU level nationwide. This isn't selective ISP blocking, it's infrastructure-level filtering across the entire Russian internet.

In April 2026, Russia's internet regulator also banned the website that tracked which VPNs still worked in the country, which tells you how seriously they're taking this.

## What No Longer Works

Being direct about what's been defeated:

- **Standard OpenVPN** on any port: TSPU identifies the OpenVPN handshake regardless of port number
- **Standard WireGuard** on 51820: WireGuard's UDP behavior and handshake are fingerprinted
- **L2TP/IPSec and PPTP**: Blocked at protocol level, largely irrelevant
- **Most Shadowsocks configurations**: Basic Shadowsocks is detectable and blocked in Russia as in China
- **V2Ray with basic VMess**: The VMess protocol has known signatures that TSPU can identify
- **Most free VPNs**: They typically use the above protocols without obfuscation layers

## What Still Works in 2026

### QUIC-Based Obfuscation

The most reliable approach for Russia is disguising VPN traffic as QUIC (HTTP/3) traffic. QUIC runs over UDP 443 and is used by YouTube, Google Search, Cloudflare, and most modern CDNs. Blocking QUIC would break enormous amounts of legitimate traffic that Russian authorities don't want to disrupt.

Mullvad has implemented WireGuard-over-QUIC obfuscation specifically for high-censorship environments. The WireGuard tunnel runs inside what looks like standard HTTP/3 packets. TSPU sees CDN traffic. This approach has remained effective in Russia through early 2026.

### Advanced TLS Obfuscation with uTLS

Proton VPN's Stealth protocol wraps WireGuard inside TLS 1.3 with a fake SNI pointing to a legitimate domain and uses uTLS to copy the exact TLS fingerprint of a specific browser version. The result is traffic that is indistinguishable from Chrome or Firefox making a normal HTTPS request.

This works because TSPU's analysis compares traffic against known-bad signatures. If your traffic perfectly matches a legitimate browser's TLS fingerprint, the DPI system has to either pass it or block all browser HTTPS, which it won't do.

### Self-Hosted Solutions

For technically capable users, self-hosted options have the best reliability because no commercial service is maintaining a target on its back:

**AmneziaWG** is a modified version of WireGuard specifically designed to defeat TSPU. It randomizes WireGuard's handshake fields, header values, and initialization packets to remove the statistical fingerprint that TSPU uses to identify WireGuard. It requires running your own server outside Russia, but it has remained consistently effective where commercial VPNs have failed.

**Amnezia VPN** is a full client that supports AmneziaWG and other anti-censorship protocols. It's open-source and the server-side component can be deployed on any VPS outside Russia.

### Commercial VPNs That Still Work

**Astrill VPN's StealthVPN** has the best commercial track record in Russia currently. StealthVPN uses SSL tunneling with active traffic shaping, randomized packet timing, and size padding to defeat statistical analysis. Astrill maintains relay infrastructure in countries close to Russia (Finland, Latvia, Estonia) for lower latency.

**Proton VPN** with Stealth protocol works in Russia. Proton is also one of the few providers that has been transparent about its technical approach, which is useful for evaluating whether it will continue to work as Russian blocking evolves.

**NordVPN** with obfuscated servers works in Russia, though performance varies by ISP. Some Russian ISPs are more aggressive than others with TSPU enforcement. NordVPN's obfuscated server network is large enough that if one server range gets blocked, there are alternatives.

## Mirror Domains and App Downloads

Roskomnadzor blocks VPN provider websites, which means you may not be able to reach nordvpn.com, protonvpn.com, or mullvad.net from inside Russia. Most major providers maintain mirror domains or alternative download URLs that are updated as the main domain gets blocked.

The practical solution: download all apps before you travel. If you're already in Russia and need to get a VPN, Telegram is often accessible (Russia has a complicated relationship with Telegram, blocking it in 2018 then unblocking it in 2020) and many VPN providers maintain Telegram channels that share current mirror URLs and APK downloads.

F-Droid is accessible in Russia and carries open-source VPN clients including Mullvad and ProtonVPN.

## Legal Status for Individuals

Using a VPN as an individual is not illegal in Russia as of 2026. The laws target VPN providers and advertisers who promote VPN services for bypassing Roskomnadzor's blocks. End users are not the enforcement target. Russian authorities have prosecuted providers and app stores that host VPN apps, not users who install and use them.

This could change. But currently, millions of Russians use VPNs daily without legal consequence.

## Our Recommendation

For travelers visiting Russia, **Proton VPN** with Stealth protocol is the strongest pick. It's effective against TSPU, based in Switzerland outside Russian jurisdiction, and has a credible no-logs policy that has been independently audited.

For residents or long-term stays, **Astrill** with StealthVPN has the most consistently reported success against TSPU across different Russian ISPs. It's more expensive but maintains anti-blocking infrastructure more actively than most competitors.

Install everything before you travel. Test the specific protocol (Stealth, StealthVPN, obfuscated) while you're still on your home network. Don't assume the app will just work when you get there.
